perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the Province of Alberta and the Parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Alberta for all disputes arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted at the Company's discretion. The Parties consent to the jurisdiction of the courts of the location where the Company resides for all disputes arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the explicit reference to Alberta law and gives the company discretion regarding governing law, potentially conflicting with Alberta's laws regarding contractual fairness and consumer protection.
perturbation.law_citation: Alberta's Consumer Protection Act, RSA 2000, c C-26.3, sections 7 and 8
perturbation.law_explanation: Alberta's Consumer Protection Act contains provisions regarding unfair terms in consumer agreements. Allowing the company unilateral discretion over the governing law and choice of jurisdiction could be deemed an unfair term, especially if it disadvantages the contractor. This change contradicts the intent of the Act to protect consumers (in this case, the contractor) from unfair contract terms.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit governing law (Alberta) and replaces it with company discretion. This contradicts the intent of consumer protection laws, as explained, because it could allow the company to choose a jurisdiction with less favorable consumer protection laws. The explanation states that Alberta's Consumer Protection Act contains provisions regarding unfair terms, and unilateral discretion over governing law could be deemed an unfair term. The snippet provided is not readable or useful, however the LLM explanation provides an adequate explanation that this change contradicts the intent of the Act to protect consumers.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against any loss or damage suffered by the Disclosing Party as a result of the failure of the Receiving Party or such other persons who are disclosed Co nfidential Information under this Agreement to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against any loss or damage suffered by the Disclosing Party as a result of gross negligence related to the confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'any loss or damage' to 'loss or damage suffered as a result of gross negligence,' the clause significantly narrows the scope of indemnification. This omission could violate Alberta's laws that mandate reasonable protection of confidential information and fair allocation of risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alberta's Law of Contract, specifically regarding reasonable expectations and good faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada, in Bhasin v Hrynew, established a duty of honest performance in contractual relations. Significantly limiting indemnification to only cases of gross negligence arguably violates this duty by shifting a disproportionate amount of risk onto the Disclosing Party, undermining reasonable expectations of protection.

perturbation.justification: The original text provided for indemnification against 'any loss or damage,' whereas the changed text limits this to 'loss or damage suffered as a result of gross negligence.' The Law Explanation states that limiting indemnification to gross negligence may violate the duty of honest performance by shifting a disproportionate amount of risk onto the Disclosing Party. This aligns with the perturbation explanation, which states that this change could violate Alberta's laws that mandate reasonable protection of confidential information. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the explanations provided indicate that the omission of 'any loss or damage' in favor of 'gross negligence' creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the protection offered to the Disclosing Party, potentially violating the duty of honest performance.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means all information relating to the Project o r the Contractor’s proposal for the Project and all information relating to the nature of either Party’s business, affairs or trade secrets, which either Party directly or indirectly receives or acquires from the other Party, or the other Party’s represent ative, either in writing or verbally (but in the case of verbal information, only that which is reduced to writing by the Disclosing Party and delivered to the Receiving Party within 10 days of the verbal disclosure) , or through observation of the Project, except information falling into any one or more of the following categories: (a) information which the Receiving Party can show was in its possession on a non - confidential basis before receipt of the information from the Disclosing Party; (b) information which is lawfully in the public domain at the time of the Receiving Party’s receipt of the information from the Disclosing Party, but not including the Company’s request for proposals for the Project; (c) information which, after the Receiving Party’s receipt of the i nformation from the Disclosing Party, becomes part of the public domain through no act of the Receiving Party or of any third party under an obligation of confidence with respect to such information, but only after such information becomes part of the publ ic domain; or (d) information which, after receipt of the information by the Receiving Party, is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party, but only after such information is so received, and provided such third party is under no obligation o f confidence with respect to such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means all information relating to the Project or the Contractor’s proposal for the Project. Excluded from confidential information is (a) information which the Receiving Party possessed before receipt.
perturbation.explanation: This drastically simplifies the definition of Confidential Information, removing key exceptions that protect the Receiving Party. Omitting clauses (b), (c), and (d) from the definition significantly broadens the scope of what is considered confidential, thereby restricting the Receiving Party’s ability to use information that is publicly available or independently obtained. This may conflict with legal principles promoting competition and free flow of information.
perturbation.law_citation: Saltman Engineering Co. Ltd. v Campbell Engineering Co. Ltd. [1963] 3 All ER 413
perturbation.law_explanation: While Alberta does not have a specific statute codifying trade secret law, it follows common law principles. The Saltman Engineering case establishes the foundation that trade secrets protection does not extend to information that is already public knowledge. Removing the exceptions for publicly available information creates a contractual obligation that is broader than what trade secret law protects, potentially stifling legitimate competition and the free flow of information, which is against public policy.

Please see ‘Frequently Asked Questions’ for details regarding the timescales for which new effects are identified and recorded on this site. 229 Rights and remedies of design right owner. U.K. (1) An infringement of design right is actionable by the design right owner. (2) In an action for infringement of design right all such relief by way of damages, injunctions, accounts or otherwise is available to the plaintiff as is available in respect of the infringement of any other property right. (3) The court may in an action for infringement of design right, having regard to all the circumstances and in particular to— (a) the flagrancy of the infringement, and (b) any benefit accruing to the defendant by reason of the infringement, award such additional damages as the justice of the case may require. (4) This section has effect subject to section 233 (innocent infringement). perturbation.justification: The modified text omits key exceptions to the definition of confidential information, specifically removing clauses that protect the Receiving Party’s ability to use information that is already in the public domain or independently obtained. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that this "significantly broadens the scope of what is considered confidential, thereby restricting the Receiving Party’s ability to use information that is publicly available or independently obtained." The Law Explanation supports this by stating that trade secret protection doesn't extend to public knowledge, and the change could stifle competition. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is about copyright, design, and patents, and seems irrelevant, the core issue is the overbroad definition of 'Confidential Information' which goes beyond reasonable trade secret protection by restricting access to information that should be freely available. The removal of the exceptions directly contradicts the principle that public information should not be subject to trade secret protection. This creates a contractual obligation that is much broader than what trade secret law protects.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to section 5.4, within 10 days of a written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall, unless otherwise precluded by any legal obligation: (a) return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s original tangible Confidential Information, together with all ta ngible copies and reproductions; (b) subject to subsection 5.3(c) , make its best efforts to delete all electronic copies of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party; (c) safeguard any electronic Confidential Information that cannot be destroyed due to the Receiving Party’s archiving practices or policies with the same degree of care as it would its own Confidential Information; and Page 4 of 6 (d) retain electronic Confidential Information described in subsection 5.3(c) only for the period it normally maintains such records, which electronic Confidential Information shall remain subject to the provisions of this Agreement until destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to section 5.4, upon a request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party may return or destroy the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the 'shall' with 'may' and removing the detailed procedure for returning or destroying confidential information, the clause gives the Receiving Party significant discretion. This omission creates uncertainty and weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information, potentially conflicting with Alberta's principles of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
perturbation.law_citation: Sattva Capital Corp v Creston Moly Corp, 2014 SCC 53
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada, in Sattva Capital Corp v Creston Moly Corp, emphasized the importance of interpreting contracts in a manner consistent with the parties' intentions and commercial reality. The modified clause, by removing the mandatory language and detailed procedure, introduces ambiguity and undermines the Disclosing Party's reasonable expectation that its confidential information will be securely returned or destroyed. This contradicts the principle of interpreting contracts in a commercially reasonable way and in accordance with the parties’ presumed intentions to protect confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: OpenAthens / Sign in Sign in Sign in with an OpenAthens account Sign-in to your Library account with OpenAthens | Connectez-vous à votre compte de bibliothèque avec OpenAthens Username Password Show Sign in opens a help message goes to a new page Problems signing in?
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates specific actions for handling confidential information ('shall return' or 'destroy'), whereas the changed text makes these actions discretionary ('may return or destroy'). This omission removes the mandatory obligation for the Receiving Party to either return or destroy the Disclosing Party's confidential information, significantly weakening the protection of that information. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this change creates uncertainty and weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information. Though Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation suggests this change is inconsistent with commercially reasonable contract interpretation, further suggesting a legal contradiction as it undermines the Disclosing Party's reasonable expectation of secure handling of confidential information.
